input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin systems** studied across the documents span **cyber-biophysical systems** (e.g., integrated cyber-physical-social-technical systems in [7]), **automotive systems** (e.g., vehicle manufacturing, assembly lines, and in-vehicle components in [8], [9], [14]), **manufacturing systems** (e.g., industrial IoT-enabled production lines adhering to **ISO 23247-1** in [10], [12], [14]), **aerospace systems** (e.g., on-orbit collision avoidance and space debris detection in [16]), and **battery systems** (e.g., energy storage units for electric vehicles in [17]). Key characteristics include:
  - **Cyber-biophysical systems**: Hybrid digital-physical models integrating **real-time sensor data**, **AI-driven simulations**, and **human-in-the-loop decision-making** (e.g., for disaster response or smart infrastructure in [7]).
  - **Automotive systems**: Modular architectures with **embedded sensors (e.g., IoT devices)**, **edge computing nodes**, and **digital threads** linking design, manufacturing, and operational phases (e.g., **ISO 23247-compliant** frameworks in [8], [9], [14]).
  - **Manufacturing systems**: **Industrial IoT (IIoT)-enabled** with **cyber-physical production systems (CPPS)**, **digital threads**, and **service-oriented architectures (SOA)** for real-time monitoring and adaptive control (e.g., **ISO 23247-1** standards in [10], [12]).
  - **Aerospace systems**: **Space-based assets** (e.g., satellites) with **collision avoidance algorithms**, **debris tracking**, and **AI-driven predictive maintenance** (e.g., adapted **ISO 23247** for orbital operations in [16]).
  - **Battery systems**: **Energy management systems (EMS)** with **state-of-charge (SoC) monitoring**, **thermal modeling**, and **degradation prediction** (e.g., **ISO 23247-aligned** frameworks in [17]).
  Suitability for digital twinning arises from **high data granularity**, **dynamic operational environments**, and **critical dependencies on real-time feedback loops** (e.g., **edge computing** for latency-sensitive applications in [14]).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin services** provided across documents include:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time sensor data ingestion** (e.g., **IoT/IIoT devices** in [8], [9], [12]) with **anomaly detection** via **machine learning (ML)** (e.g., **AI-driven simulation** in [7], [15]).
    - **State estimation** (e.g., **battery SoC/health monitoring** in [17], **vehicle component diagnostics** in [8]).
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Predictive maintenance** using **digital twin-driven prognostic models** (e.g., **aerospace debris avoidance** in [16], **manufacturing process optimization** via **ISO 23247-1** in [10]).
    - **Resource allocation** (e.g., **edge computing-based load balancing** for digital twin workloads in [14]).
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Physics-based and data-driven simulations** (e.g., **AI simulation frameworks** in [15], **thermal/structural degradation models** for batteries in [17]).
    - **Failure mode prediction** (e.g., **automotive component wear** in [9], **space debris collision risks** in [16]).
  - **Control**:
    - **Closed-loop control** via **digital twin feedback** (e.g., **adaptive manufacturing control** in [12], **autonomous vehicle path planning** in [8]).
    - **Automated decision-making** (e.g., **cyber-biophysical system response** to dynamic events in [7]).
  - **Visualization**:
    - **3D/4D digital replicas** with **interactive dashboards** (e.g., **ISO 23247-compliant** visualization layers in [10], [14]).
    - **Augmented Reality (AR)/Virtual Reality (VR)** for **remote operation** (e.g., **aerospace maintenance** in [16]).
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers** (e.g., **design/manufacturing optimization** in [8], [9]).
    - **Operators** (e.g., **real-time control** in [7], [16]).
    - **Maintenance teams** (e.g., **predictive analytics** in [10], [17]).
    - **Decision-makers** (e.g., **strategic planning** via **digital twin insights** in [12], [15]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin implementation across documents include:
  - **Frameworks/Standards**:
    - **ISO 23247-1** (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, including **digital threads**, **data models**, and **interoperability guidelines** in [10], [12], [14], [16], [17]).
    - **ISO/IEC 30173** (Digital Twin concepts and terminology, defining **lifecycle phases**, **data exchange protocols**, and **semantic interoperability** in [11]).
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Edge computing platforms** (e.g., **ISO 23247-aligned edge nodes** for **low-latency data processing** in [14]).
    - **Simulation engines**:
      - **Physics-based simulators** (e.g., **thermal/structural models** for batteries in [17]).
      - **AI/ML-driven simulators** (e.g., **digital twin-based AI frameworks** in [15], **prognostic algorithms** in [7], [16]).
    - **Digital Twin development tools**:
      - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) tools** (e.g., **ACM/IEEE MODELS conference focus** in [7]).
      - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** for **modular Digital Twin components** (e.g., **ISO 23247-compliant microservices** in [12]).
  - **Databases/Storage**:
    - **Time-series databases** (e.g., **IoT sensor data storage** in [8], [9]).
    - **Semantic data lakes** (e.g., **ontology-based knowledge graphs** for **interoperability** in [11], [13]).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Digital Threads**: End-to-end **data flows** linking **design, manufacturing, and operation** (e.g., **automotive lifecycle** in [8], [9]).
    - **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) integration**: **Real-time synchronization** between **physical assets** and **digital replicas** (e.g., **IIoT-enabled manufacturing** in [12]).
    - **Adaptation layers**: **Domain-specific extensions** (e.g., **aerospace collision avoidance** in [16], **battery thermal management** in [17]).
    - **Security/Privacy**: **Blockchain for data integrity** (implied in [13] for **Systems of Twinned Systems**), **encryption protocols** for **sensitive IoT data** (e.g., [8], [9]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin System (DTS) enforces a **100 ms time synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination. This temporal granularity is mandated by hardware limitations and aligns with safety-critical vehicular applications, though stricter synchronization (e.g., <100 ms) is required by some standards. The system leverages **clock synchronization mechanisms** to ensure temporal alignment across distributed components, particularly for real-time services like sensor data processing, actuation, and device communication. Latency requirements are implicitly constrained by the 100 ms threshold, which governs the maximum acceptable delay for time-sensitive operations within the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity. No explicit mention of multi-tiered time scales (e.g., microsecond-level for control vs. millisecond-level for visualization) is provided, but the 100 ms constraint applies uniformly to all time-critical services.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS adopts a **decentralized architecture** with **three distinct hierarchical entities**, each deployed on separate hardware components: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*. This structure diverges from ISO 23247 by introducing the *Physical Twin Entity* as a dedicated container for inherent system resources (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and general automotive functionalities like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*, whereas the standard’s closest equivalent (OME) hosts only resource-specific features. Multiplicities are not explicitly discussed for individual Digital Twin instances; however, the entity-based design suggests **modular scalability** where each entity can be instantiated independently. Coordination between entities is managed via **ROS2-based communication** (e.g., ROS2 packages/nodes) and adherence to the ISO 23247 reference architecture, though deviations in ownership and engineering processes may arise due to hardware-specific deployments. No centralized orchestration layer is described; instead, responsibilities are partitioned by entity (e.g., *User Interface FE* split into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DTS orchestrates its constellation through a **ROS2-based microservices architecture**, where each functional entity (*Physical Twin*, *Digital Twin*, *User*) is implemented as an **independent ROS2 package** containing one or more ROS2 nodes. This design aligns with the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)** but introduces deviations, such as the *Physical Twin Entity* hosting general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) beyond resource-specific operations. Component integration is achieved via **ROS2 inter-node communication**, enabling real-time data exchange between sensors/actuators (Physical Twin), data management/control mechanisms (Digital Twin), and user-facing interfaces (User Entity). Resource management is implicit in the entity-based structure, with hardware-specific deployments dictating ownership of functionalities. No explicit system-wide coordination protocol (e.g., consensus algorithms, event-driven buses) is mentioned; instead, the architecture relies on **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model** and the ISO 23247 RA’s entity relationships for orchestration. The absence of certain ISO 23247 Function Elements (FEs) and the splitting of the *User Interface FE* into subcomponents further tailor the constellation to the system’s requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture**, which defines an **entity-based structure** where the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** digitally represents the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**—a physical entity such as equipment, facilities, products, or processes. The DTE is composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which may include **computational models** for digital representation, though the standard does not prescribe specific model types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, or analytical). Data components are managed through **Data Collection and Device Control Entities**, which interface with OMEs to gather real-time operational data. The **User Entity** provides human-interpretable interfaces for monitoring and control, implying data transformation into actionable formats. Model relationships are implicit in the entity-based architecture, where FEs collaborate to fulfill specific tasks (e.g., actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing). The standard does not specify data sources, management approaches, or model relationships beyond the entity-based framework, leaving implementation details (e.g., OPC UA, proprietary protocols) to the developer's discretion.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247 standard** does not explicitly detail **fidelity requirements** (e.g., resolution, granularity) for Digital Twin models or data. However, the **entity-based architecture** implies that validation and verification (V&V) must align with the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**'s operational accuracy. While the standard does not prescribe specific **uncertainty quantification methods** or **error correction mechanisms**, it establishes a framework where **Data Collection and Device Control Entities** must ensure data integrity for real-time monitoring and control. Quality assurance processes are indirectly addressed through the **User Entity**, which provides interfaces for human oversight, suggesting a reliance on **user-driven validation** of model outputs. The standard does not mention **sampling frequencies, precision specifications, or formal V&V methodologies** (e.g., model calibration, statistical testing), leaving these as implementation-dependent considerations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves the following structured data flows:

1. **Data Types Transmitted**:
   - **LiDAR Point Cloud Data**: Raw point cloud data from LiDAR sensors, processed via the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software component to generate structured object tracks and environmental representations.
   - **Vehicle State Data**: Real-time telemetry from the vehicle (e.g., speed, position, orientation) sourced from the **Vehicle State** functional entity (FE).
   - **Actuation Data**: Motor and servo actuation commands/feedback (e.g., steering angle, motor torque) from the **Motor** and **Steering Servo Actuation FE**.
   - **Object Tracks & Driving Path**: Processed LiDAR-derived object tracks and projected driving paths (e.g., for Adaptive Cruise Control, ACC) sent to the **Controlling FE** for visualization and decision-making.

2. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-Entity Communication**: Wi-Fi network using **UDP** in the transport layer for data transmission between the **Physical Twin Entity** (sensors/vehicle) and **Digital Twin Entity** (software components).
   - **Intra-Entity Communication**: **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** network for communication between software components within the Digital Twin Entity.

3. **Transmission Frequencies**:
   - Not explicitly specified in the document, but implied to be **real-time** for critical data (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state) to support live 3D visualization and ACC functionality.

4. **Event Triggers**:
   - **Sensor Data Acquisition**: Triggered by LiDAR scans, motor/servo state changes, or vehicle state updates.
   - **Data Processing Events**: Triggered by raw data ingestion into the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** component or **Data Pre-Processing FE**.
   - **Visualization Updates**: Triggered by changes in vehicle state, object tracks, or driving path data (e.g., for ACC display in Fig. 3a).

5. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - **Preprocessing Steps**:
     - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**: Converts raw LiDAR points (2D plane representation) into structured object tracks and environmental maps.
     - **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Likely includes noise filtering, coordinate transformation, and feature extraction for downstream components.
   - **Data Translation Component**: Ensures **serialization**, **message type alignment**, and **unit conversions** for all data streams to maintain consistency across the system.

6. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - **Data Translation FE**: Acts as a middleware to align message formats and perform unit conversions (e.g., converting LiDAR coordinates to a standardized frame).
   - **ROS2 Network**: Facilitates modular integration of software components (e.g., **Presentation FE**, **Reporting FE**, **Controlling FE**) by publishing/subcribing to standardized topics.
   - **Wi-Fi/UDP Transport**: Enables low-latency, high-throughput data transfer between physical sensors and digital processing units.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves control flows from the **Digital Twin Entity** to the **Physical Twin Entity**, governed by the following technical specifications:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **Motor Actuation Commands**: Generated by the **Controlling FE** (e.g., for ACC or autonomous driving) and sent to the **Motor Actuation FE** to adjust throttle or braking.
   - **Steering Servo Commands**: Sent from the **Controlling FE** to the **Steering Servo Actuation FE** to modify steering angle.
   - **Vehicle State Overrides**: Potential commands to adjust vehicle parameters (e.g., dynamic stability control) via the **Vehicle State FE**.

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - Standardized via the **Data Translation FE**, which ensures **serialization** and **message type alignment** for control signals (e.g., ROS2 message formats for motor/servo commands).
   - Likely includes **structured payloads** with fields for command type, target values (e.g., steering angle in degrees), and execution parameters.

3. **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
   - **Not explicitly detailed in the document**, but implied by the **Data Translation FE**’s role in ensuring message integrity and consistency.
   - Potential reliance on **ROS2’s built-in topic-based communication** for basic validation (e.g., message schema compliance).

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Actuation Feedback Loop**: The **Motor** and **Steering Servo Actuation FE** provide real-time feedback (e.g., actual motor torque, steering angle) back to the **Controlling FE** for closed-loop control.
   - **Vehicle State Updates**: The **Vehicle State FE** continuously reports physical state changes (e.g., speed, position) to the Digital Twin for validation against commanded actions.

5. **Conflict Resolution**:
   - **Not explicitly specified**, but the **Controlling FE** (e.g., ACC logic) likely implements priority-based arbitration for conflicting commands (e.g., emergency braking vs. steering adjustments).

6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - Implied via **feedback loops** from the **Actuation FE** to the **Controlling FE**, ensuring commands are executed as intended (e.g., confirmed steering angle matches the commanded value).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this Digital Twin System (DTS) is defined by the following specifications:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Intra-Entity (Software Components)**: **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** network for publish-subscribe communication between software components (e.g., **Presentation FE**, **Controlling FE**, **Data Translation FE**).
   - **Inter-Entity (Physical-Digital)**: **Wi-Fi network using UDP** in the transport layer for low-latency data exchange between the **Physical Twin Entity** (sensors/vehicle) and **Digital Twin Entity** (software).

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Hierarchical Model**:
     - **Physical Twin Entity**: Sources raw data (LiDAR, motor/servo states, vehicle telemetry).
     - **Digital Twin Entity**: Hosts software components (e.g., **Data Pre-Processing FE**, **Controlling FE**, **Presentation FE**) communicating via ROS2.
     - **User Entity**: Interfaces with the Digital Twin via **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** (Fig. 3).
   - **Data Flow Path**: Sensor data → **Data Pre-Processing FE** → **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** → **Controlling FE** → **Presentation FE** → **User Interface**.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **Not explicitly quantified**, but implied to be **low-latency** for real-time applications (e.g., LiDAR point cloud processing, ACC control).
   - **UDP Transport**: Prioritizes speed over reliability for time-critical data (e.g., vehicle state updates).

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **Data Translation FE**: Ensures **message consistency** and **unit conversion** across components, reducing protocol mismatches.
   - **ROS2 Topics**: May include **redundant publishers** or **retry mechanisms** for critical data (e.g., vehicle state).
   - **Wi-Fi Redundancy**: Not specified, but UDP’s stateless nature may require application-layer recovery for dropped packets.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Not explicitly detailed in the document**. Potential reliance on:
     - **ROS2 Security Extensions** (e.g., TLS for encrypted communication between components).
     - **Wi-Fi Encryption** (e.g., WPA3) for inter-entity communication.
     - **Authentication**: Implied via **Data Translation FE**’s role in validating message formats, though no specific mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin System (DTS) is characterized by the following technical and operational aspects:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Edge Deployment**: Implied by the **on-board processing** of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR clustering, motor/servo control) to minimize latency.
   - **On-Premise (Vehicle-Integrated)**: The **Physical Twin Entity** (sensors, actuators) and **Digital Twin Entity** (software components) are co-located within the vehicle, suggesting a **vehicle-centric deployment model**.
   - **Cloud Not Explicitly Mentioned**: No evidence of cloud hosting; all processing appears localized to the vehicle’s embedded systems.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - **Computational**:
     - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**: Requires significant processing power for real-time LiDAR data analysis (e.g., GPU acceleration for point cloud operations).
     - **Controlling FE**: Demands low-latency computation for ACC logic and actuator control.
     - **Data Translation FE**: Lightweight but must handle high-throughput data serialization.
   - **Storage**:
     - **Data Storage FE**: Likely stores processed data (e.g., object tracks, reports) for short-term analysis or logging.
     - **No Cloud Storage Specified**: Storage appears localized, with potential for **on-vehicle flash memory** or **SSD** for high-speed access.

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Modular ROS2 Architecture**: Enables horizontal scaling by adding redundant nodes or components (e.g., multiple **Controlling FE** instances for different driving modes).
   - **Component-Based Design**: Software services (e.g., **Presentation FE**, **Reporting FE**) can be scaled independently based on demand.
   - **Not Explicitly Distributed**: No mention of distributed computing (e.g., Kubernetes) or cloud scaling; scalability is limited to vehicle-integrated resources.

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Single-Vehicle Deployment**: Primarily designed for **individual vehicle use cases** (e.g., autonomous driving, ADAS).
   - **No Multi-Vehicle or Fleet Scaling**: No evidence of support for distributed Digital Twins across multiple vehicles or centralized orchestration.

5. **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
   - **Redundancy**: Implied via **feedback loops** (e.g., actuation confirmation) and **ROS2’s fault-tolerant design** (e.g., node restarts).
   - **Real-Time Constraints**: Critical for **safety-critical applications** (e.g., ACC), but no explicit redundancy (e.g., backup sensors or processors) is described.
   - **Data Persistence**: **Reporting FE** suggests periodic data logging, but no high-availability storage mechanisms (e.g., RAID, replication) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) development process, as outlined in **ISO 23247 Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, follows an **entity-based framework** where the system is decomposed into four core **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes—each digitally represented by a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**. The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** handles real-time data acquisition and actuation of the OME, while the **User Entity** provides interfaces for monitoring and control. **Functional Entities (FEs)** within each entity execute specific tasks (e.g., data transformation, human-interpretable visualization, or OME actuation), though the standard does not prescribe implementation methods (e.g., OPC UA could be used for communication). **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed, but the modular entity structure implies incremental updates to FEs or DTEs without disrupting the entire system. **Testing and validation** are implied through the **entity-based RA**, where each FE’s functionality must align with its OME’s operational requirements, though no specific methodologies (e.g., unit testing, simulation) are referenced.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247 standard**, specifically **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, which defines an **entity-based framework** for manufacturing DTs. Key compliance elements include:
  - **Entity-based architecture**: Mandates four core entities (**Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**, and **User Entity**), each with defined responsibilities.
  - **Interoperability**: While not explicitly prescribed, the standard implies compatibility with protocols like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for data exchange between entities (e.g., DTE ↔ Data Collection Entity).
  - **Certification**: No explicit certification requirements are mentioned, but adherence to ISO 23247 ensures alignment with **manufacturing-specific DT frameworks**.
  - **Compliance frameworks**: The standard provides a **modular, entity-centric** approach, ensuring traceability and consistency across DT implementations. **Information exchange** (ISO 23247 Part 4) is referenced but not detailed in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system, particularly in the context of **ISO 26262-1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety)**, adheres to **functional safety standards** for safety-critical systems, including **automotive applications** (e.g., adaptive cruise control systems as referenced in **ISO 15622:2018**). This involves:
  - **Risk assessment methodologies** aligned with **ISO 26262**, which defines **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)** for system components, ensuring compliance with **safety requirements specifications** and **hazard analysis** (e.g., FMEA, FTA).
  - **Fail-safe mechanisms** and **redundancy protocols** to mitigate system failures, particularly in **safety-critical subsystems** (e.g., braking, steering, or communication modules).
  - **Cybersecurity protocols** implicitly tied to **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services** as outlined in **3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0**, which mandates **secure communication channels** (e.g., **authentication, encryption, and integrity protection**) for **inter-vehicle and infrastructure communication** to prevent unauthorized access or data tampering.
  - **Access control mechanisms** for **model-driven architectures** (as discussed in **Dalibor et al., 2020**), where **role-based permissions** and **digital twin cockpit interfaces** enforce **least-privilege access** to sensitive system states or control parameters.
  - **Threat mitigation strategies** for **real-time system interactions**, including **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** and **secure boot processes** to ensure **tamper-proof execution** of safety-critical functions.
  - **Compliance with automotive-grade security standards** (e.g., **ISO/SAE 21434** for cybersecurity engineering, though not explicitly cited, is implied by the context of **ISO 26262** and **3GPP V2X requirements**).

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
